speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two of the interview of Jack Arnel on August 29, 1996. Mr. Arnel, you described the view from your room in Vilna as being gardens and a Russian Orthodox Church. What was your relationship like with the-- with the people who you saw walking in the garden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was absolutely wonderful. They were very loving and wonderful people. I remember most of the priests made a special effort to wave to us and say hello, and ask how we are. One in particular, he was-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH] David. He was-- name was Father David. And he wore these long black robes, you know, and-- and the special hats. He befriended us so much that at times, mother invited him to come Sunday for tea and biscuits. And he would come with his long robe. And I-- I would sit on his lap and he would-- he would actually hug me and tell me how much he loves me. And so that the next morning before I woke up to go to school, he would throw this apples and pears, and-- and-- and flowers, into the-- up the second floor, right through-- through the windows. And [SIGHS] he really cared for us very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your relationship change when-- when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the relationship did not change. But the circumstances have changed. And I'll tell you a little bit more about it as we go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first learn about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I heard-- my father used to listen to the news when-- 1939 when the Germans attacked Poland, the war broke out in 1939. And they ran a-- actually, I should say that before the war broke out, Hitler and Stalin had an agreement, just about a week before. And they agreed that when the Germans will run over Poland, that part of it is going to be taken over by Russia. And as it turned out, that Vilna, and Bialostocka, and some of the eastern part of Poland was designated as a part that the Russians would take over. And so a week later, after this agreement-- actually, the agreement was signed by Ribbentrop and Molotov. And they signed this agreement and a week later, the Germans attacked Poland. And Poland didn't last long. They had a nice army and cavalry and horses and all that, which was no match to the Russian panzers and tanks and airplanes. And we didn't-- I think it lasted at the most two weeks. They overran the entire country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And although the Russians moved in into our town. And-- Did the Russian army actually come into Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: The actually Russian army come into the Vilna, with their tanks and with their armed forces. And that was when the-- the whole beautiful life that I had before ended. Although it was not as bad with the-- living with the Russians as it-- as later on we found out with the Germans. But nonetheless, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what was it like with the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. [SIGHS] 1941, I believe it was June of 1941, when the Russians came into our town. And they took over the administration of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was in 1941 or 1939 that the Russians came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. It was 1941. No. I'm sorry, 1939. Correct. Yes. 1939 is when the Russians came in. And they-- of course, the Russians were coming from a-- it was the Soviet Union. It was a communist state, also a dictatorial state. And under their constitution, and there-- there is no private ownership. And so they liquidated a lot of private businesses. It's a matter of fact, they-- like my father's fur shop, and many other furriers-- Vilna was very known for a lot of fur. The-- the fur industry was very big there. A lot of fur shops, a lot of furriers. And they decreed that all the furriers must donate all their equipment to the one big factory that they're going to make. And all the owners will now be part of the workforce, working for the state. And so they took away all the machinery and all the equipment. And they built-- they actually put it all in a one big building where all the furriers used to go to work. And my father, of course, was working there as well. He was no longer a boss, his own-- owner. He was just like another worker. But they made him a manager because of the fact that he was so skillful in the trade. He-- [SIGHS] he was-- the pressure was on. He was under a lot of stress. He was not allowed to be late a few minutes. He was not-- it was very restricted. The pay was much smaller. And, of course, all the luxuries that we enjoyed before the war were gone. There were no more [NON-ENGLISH]. There were no more trips. There were no more-- except-- there were no more-- a lot of things were taken away. And there were no private schools. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still have a maid? A governess?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not anymore. After that, they-- they left. And it was against the-- the-- what the-- what the Soviet Union stood for. It would be considered abuse, or whatever. So we continued our lives based on the new way. We-- the Russians tried very hard to indoctrinate the youth into their communist system. They wanted the young children from school to become Pioneers. And I had to go, now, to a public school, public school number 38. And we were taught Russian language. And everything, all the other things, a lot of propaganda on behalf of the Soviet Union was put into effect. They concentrated very much on the youth. And they used to have a lot of trucks come and pick up the youth in the streets and-- and drive them to nice places, to the circus, or whatever. The state paid for it, just get them on their good side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become a Pioneer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a Pioneer for a little bit. Actually, I had not much of a choice. You know what I mean? I was a kid in the school, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain what a Pioneer is.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Pioneer is a young person in a school who is joining-- actually, from the Pioneer, you become a-- as you grow older, you become a Komsomol. And from Komsomol, you become a-- a member of the Communist Party, actually. That what it entailed. But the indoctrination, they wanted as young as you-- the younger, the better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it like a Boy Scout organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, exactly. Yes. It is very similar to a Boy Scout.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had to wear a-- a red kerchief on our neck, with a-- with a ring around there. And there was a certain-- I don't recall it, exactly. But you had to give an answer when they ever asked, how come you wear this red kerchief? And then you have to explain that. It's on behalf of the-- of the country of the Soviet Union and the communism and all that. This is really sort of snuck in to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And life was, generally speaking, good. But not as good as it was. It was different. We had to adjust to many things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother still have her shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she had somewhat. But it sort of petered out, really. Because people could not afford anymore. People had no interest anymore. I recall the Russians used to have big screen movies out in the streets and call the-- the people, population to see. And all you saw was, the-- they were showing the revolution of 1917, and explaining what has transpired, and tell them where-- that the future is so great, and all that. [SIGHS] We still had enough to eat, and to-- and to enjoy life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still have the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: The synagogue was really-- the synagogues were there. But we did not attend anymore. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a way, it was sort of like we were a little afraid. You know? The-- the NKVD at that time, they kept records of who is what. And we didn't want to rock the boat, so to speak. And did the Betar stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, actually, no. They continued a little bit more. They were, it's a matter of fact, at the time when the Russians occupied Vilna in 1939, the-- the other part of Poland that was occupied by Germany, and they began to suffer all the indignations and all the-- the hardships and the-- and the slaughter, a lot of-- quite a few of those had managed to come to Vilna, the-- especially young boys and girls. They managed to run away from there. And they formed the [NON-ENGLISH] groups. They were transient in Vilna who with a-- with a-- a hope to reach Israel or Palestine at the time, any given time. And they wanted to go to Palestine to work and build, and I guess fight for an independent country. And so they were transients and they were in there. And it was rather very difficult for them to get out. In the meantime, they did all kinds of menial works there, to support themselves. And so they were still run by the Zionist organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those people who came to Vilna, escaping from Western Poland, did they tell you what was going on in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They brought that horrible news of what was going on. And even though we-- we heard the news with-- and we felt that the Russian might-- the Soviet Union-- with all its tanks and all that are very strong. And we felt sort of, in a way, secure. The mere fact that the Germans occupied the Western part of Poland and-- and the Russians came in on the eastern side of-- we felt that perhaps we are in a safe zone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And even if we didn't feel safe and wanted to get out of there, it-- it wasn't easy. I mean, Vilna-- Vilna had between 60,000 or 70,000 Jews. And how many were able to get out? Even if they tried their hardest way was they had to go through the Soviet Union and all the-- it was practically impossible. So with these new arrivals from Poland, then, with the Communist regime, was there any change in the relationship between the Jews of Vilna and the non-Jews, or the Gentiles, of Vilna at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, not really. There were a lot of Jewish people who were Communists themselves, you know. And as a matter of fact, the more Jewish people were involved in-- in the government jobs at that time. And I would imagine Poles may have resented that, you know, to-- in a way. But the Communist Party had Poles and Jews, everybody in it. It was a matter of, I guess on the Polish part, to suppress their feelings at the time, since they were under the occupation of the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that went on until when? The Russian occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was really something. The Russians came into our town in-- can't remember exactly the date. But it was 1939. June 1939, I believe. And they-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] couple of weeks later or so, they gave the entire town of Vilna to the Lithuanians. Because the Lithuanians always said that Vilna is their-- their city, belongs to Lithuania, from way back in-- in history. They said that [? Vilnius-- ?] they called it [? Vilnius, ?] actually. It was their capital hundreds of years ago, and all that. And they always had a claim on Vilna. So the Russians gave them Vilna. And the Lithuanian army came in and the Russians withdrew only to the outskirts of Vilna. The Lithuanian army came in on bicycles. And it was really funny to see them come in on bicycles after the Russians came in with their tanks. But it didn't last long. A little while later, the Russians took over all of Lithuania. So it was going back and forth like that. And we lived under the regime of the Lithuanians for awhile, which was not much of a change at that time. Because the Russians took right over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the Russians came back. And how long did the Russian occupation last?

speaker: interviewee
text: One year. From-- I believe, till the Germans attacked. And that was in 1941. I believe it was June of 1941. When the Germans--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your recollection of the attack?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It was a very somber and very frightening time. When we heard that the Germans-- actually, we didn't know that the Germans were attacking until we heard the bombs falling on-- on the town. At night, suddenly, we heard all these explosions. And my father jumped out of bed and got out to speak to the neighbors and all that. And we knew that they were-- that the Germans are attacking. We-- because we saw also the-- some of the Russian soldiers running in our streets. And as a matter of fact, they were in such disarray and they were actually escaping from the German attack.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next morning, we got up. It was silence. It was quiet. And I went down into the courtyard. And I saw some of my friends, the Polish boys, that were very close friends of mine. We walked out on the street a little bit further.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly a German-- a German motorcycle-- on a motorcycle-- three Germans were on a motorcycle with a-- with a cab that they had on the side. And they had their machine guns on their backs. Stopped in front of us. We must have been, maybe, six or seven boys all together. I was the only Jewish boy--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Well, it was 1941. I was born in 1929. I was 12 years old. And my friends, they didn't understand any German. But since I spoke Yiddish, I was able to understand what they were saying. So one of the Germans said to them, in-- in-- in German, he said that, all the Juden will be killed. That all the Jews will be killed. Well, I felt a tremendous weakness in my knees. And as they stood there talking, I just walked away quietly. And then I ran home. And I ran into my room. And I fell on the-- onto my bed. And I was sobbing uncontrollably.

speaker: interviewer
text: I couldn't understand it. I couldn't understand. Why would they say a thing like this? And my mother and my father came to me, trying to console me, to try to help me. And I, I just kept asking, why did he say that? Why? It just blew my mind. Couldn't understand it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] And so, the real tragedy has begun. The tragedy of-- as a 12-year-old, I was rather young. I was brought on in a protective environment. It hit me like a like an atomic bomb. It-- I just could not believe the things that have developed in there. And it had weighed a tremendous burden on my mind and on my heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Germans immediately occupy Vilna right after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They were-- it's a matter of days. They were right in. As a matter of fact, they occupied Vilna, I believe it was June of 1941. June of 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the first thing they did, that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as soon as they [SIGHS] got in, within a few weeks, the first-- the first command came from the-- they established their own government there, Vilna. And all the different divisions of the German Nazis, they-- they-- they had the Einsatzgruppen, and they had the-- the-- the SD, the-- the police. And they had all kinds of different divisions, all murderous divisions. And the-- the first thing they did was an order that all Jews must wear the yellow badge, with a J inside, inscribed. One on the front and one in the back. That was the very first indignation. That was to separate us from everybody else. That was to insult us. To-- most importantly, to have control over us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and your family all wear the yellow badge?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the Jews had to wear that. Then they made other restriction that we were not allowed to work on the-- walk on the sidewalks. We were allowed only to work next the sidewalk, in the street. And then they restricted the time of when we could walk, especially to go out shop for food or anything like this. And they did not allow the Jews to show up in the morning-- not until the hours-- up until the hours of, I believe it was 3 o'clock in the afternoon. I mean, a 3:00 or-- and 5:00, whatever was left over, the Jews were allowed to go into the store and-- and buy something. And after 6:00, 6 o'clock, I think there's a curfew. They wouldn't allow any Jews walking at all in the streets. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get your yellow star? Did you have to make it yourself? Did they give it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You had to-- to make it yourself. As long as it was yellow fabric. It had to be sewn on. They gave you exact dimensions, how big it should be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did your mother make those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Every Jew had to make this. And-- [SIGHS] and then after that, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what did your friends do? Your polish friends that you played with every day, when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They just stayed away. I wasn't anymore in the court playing. I was-- I was in the house, for the most part. School ended. There was no more schooling for me. And my father still had to go to work in the factory. He had a, like, approximately, six or seven blocks' walk to the factory from where we lived. And he still was manager, running the factory, making the furs over there. It's a matter of fact that the Germans, when they discovered that fur factory, they took it over. They put it in one of their Germans in charge of it. And they thought that they would need that as a means of producing fur coats and fur vests for the German army, which was fighting in the east, the Russian army, and the very severe cold winters over there, they felt that this was a-- an absolute necessity. And for the most part, in that factory, were all, I would say, maybe 80% of Jewish furriers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people worked in that factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, I would imagine as far as furriers, maybe 300 at the time. And so my father kept going to work and they-- that factory was now under the jurisdiction of the Wehermacht. The-- not the Gestapo, but the-- the Wehrmacht was a different-- a different division.

speaker: interviewer
text: The regular army?

speaker: interviewee
text: The regular army. And they thought that this was a-- actually that this factory and the people that worked in there, who were actually contributing to the war effort. And so that these are nutzliche Juden. My father was a nutzliche Jude, which meant he was an important Jew who contributed to the effort by being there and manufacturing the fur vests for the German army. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to other people in Vilna who were not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. I was-- I was-- got-- just going to tell you-- Vilna had, as I said before, between around 65,000 or 66,000 inhabitants. Right after the-- the yellow stars had to be worn and the walking, the Germans, the Einsatzkommando, that group came in. And they started rounding up Jews, catching all-- especially males mostly, right from the streets walking, regardless of where, what and how, or what time. They just kept scooping them up and taking them to the-- to the city jail, which was on Lukishki. It was called Lukishki Jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Lukishki?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-U-K-I-S-H-K-I, I believe. Lukishki. And right from the beginning, the first shot, they rounded up maybe 1,500 or 2,000 Jewish men. They took them there and we didn't know what happened to them. They disappeared. We-- later on, we found out that the town of Ponar, which is, I don't know exactly how many miles away from Vilna, maybe 50 miles or so from Vilna, or 40 miles. I don't-- I'm not sure about the mileage. But that became the graveyard for the Vilna Jews. This is where 98% of the Vilna Jews were slaughtered over the years. But what they did-- what happened was, we didn't know that anything that has happened. All we knew is that they may have been rounded up and sent to do work or help the German army carry their ammunitions. We were-- just surmised different things. But nobody could believe that they were taken to Ponar and actually shot. And we-- we discovered later on that they were shot. They would bring them into a holding place in Ponar there, there was a big fence, a big area they dug out big pits. And they put boards along the edges. And they would march-- they would bring the people into a holding-- stage area. And then they would march 10 people at a time onto the boards. And must tell you that the Lithuanian population, the Lithuanians, were the most brutal ones. They were the most cooperative with the-- with the-- with the Germans. And they participated in the slaughter. They were extremely brutal. And 10 people at a time were shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Polish population that lived in the vicinity there heard the shots. They also didn't know at the-- in the beginning, what to make of it. You know? But that's how it began.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- there was this-- the-- the Germans followed the dictates of the final solution. The final solution in [NON-ENGLISH] was-- was declared that all Jews have to be exterminated. And the methods and the-- and the-- and the-- the time slot allowed was up to the individual heads of the Gestapo and the Nazis in the area. [SIGHS] Then there came a time after they have had this-- I think that before they announced the formation of the ghetto, probably around 5,000 or 6,000 Jewish men were rounded up at different actions like this here, and slaughtered in Ponar.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of the second tape of the interview of Jack Arnel.
